name: Racket
summary: Scheme-descended Lisp where modules are phase-aware language-extension boundaries; for-syntax and module phases tie imports to the macro-expansion model; the #lang mechanism lets a source file declare which language it is written in, making modules into language-construction substrate.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Racket (PLT, 1995+; the language formerly known as PLT Scheme) is the most important example in the typed-and-phase-aware family for a different reason from OCaml or SML: it treats modules as **phase-aware language-extension boundaries**. A Racket module is not only a namespace and compilation unit; it is also part of the language's macro-expansion model.

sections.title: Phase-aware imports — for-syntax and module visiting
sections.content:
Imports can be shifted across phases with forms like `for-syntax`, and modules can be instantiated or visited at different phase levels depending on whether they are needed for runtime evaluation or compile-time expansion.

This is a qualitatively different design point from both the static graph systems and the ML-style typed module systems. In Racket, modules participate directly in the language's metaprogramming semantics. The distinction between compile-time and runtime is not an implementation detail hidden behind proc-macro crates or compiler internals; it is part of the programmer-visible module language.

The complexity has multiple dimensions: multiple phase levels, module instantiation vs visiting, different import modes for compile-time and runtime, and a tighter coupling between the expander and the module system than most languages ever attempt.

sections.title: #lang — modules as language-construction substrate
sections.content:
The `#lang` mechanism pushes module-as-extension-boundary even further. A source file can declare not only "which module am I in?" but effectively "which language is this file written in?" That makes modules into language-construction boundaries. Racket's module system is therefore one of the strongest examples anywhere of **modules as extension substrate**, not just code organization.

Production `#lang` ecosystems include Typed Racket (typed dialect), Scribble (documentation), Pollen (publishing DSL), and dozens of pedagogical and research dialects. Each is implemented as a module that exports a reader and a set of bindings that the host file uses as its language.

sections.title: Design warning and inspiration
sections.content:
Racket is not primarily a candidate for direct imitation. It is a warning and an inspiration. If a language ever wants macros, DSLs, or language-as-library features at this level, then **module phases must be designed deliberately**. If that power is not wanted now, then Racket is evidence that it is often better deferred than approximated halfway.

For a language designer, the takeaway is: don't bolt phase-aware imports onto an existing module system later. The phase tower interacts with module elaboration, name resolution, hygiene, and incremental compilation in ways that are very hard to retrofit.
